comments.title: Inconsistent use of NA relation in training description
comments.quote: (2) For templates where _l_ = _|r|_ , since there is no relation between ( _s, o_ ) under _T_ ( _s, o_ ; _l_ ), models are asked to reconstruct the NA relation.
comments.explanation: At first glance, this sentence appears contradictory: it says 'for templates where _l = |r|_' — which should match the length of the ground-truth relation r — and yet claims 'there is no relation', which conflicts with the premise that we have a relational triplet (s, r, o). From the context, it is clear the authors intend two distinct cases: (1) when the relation exists, reconstruct r; and (2) when it doesn't, reconstruct NA. But the logic here is reversed. The correct condition should be: when l ≠ |r| (e.g., for templates of wrong length), or when no relation exists at all, then predict NA. However, as written, this suggests that even for the correct-length template of an existing relation, the model should predict NA, which would be incorrect. The prior sentence correctly handles l = |r| by reconstructing ri. This statement contradicts it. The confusion arises because the phrase 'since there is no relation between (s, o) under T(s, o; l)' implies that relation existence depends on the template, which is not how the task is framed — relations are image-grounded, not template-defined. This misstatement could mislead readers about the training procedure. Although the surrounding context provides some clarity, the logical inconsistency remains unresolved and undermines the precise specification of training dynamics.

comments.title: Ambiguity in final color set construction via CPS
comments.quote: Similarly, we can obtain the visual appearances according to the largest decoding score, resulting in the color set: _C_ = _{_ ( _cv, cw_ ) _|cv_ = arg max _c_ ˆ _iv∈C_ ˆ _v s_ (ˆ _c[i] v[, c][j] w_[)] _[, c][j] w[∈C][w][}]_.
comments.explanation: The procedure for constructing C is underspecified. The text says first we select C_w by choosing for each visual candidate cˆv[i] the cˆw[j] that maximizes s(cˆv[i], cˆw[j]). That yields a mapping from visual appearances to color texts. Then the authors say 'similarly' we obtain visual appearances to complete the pairs in C. But how exactly? The formula suggests that for each already-selected c_j^w in C_w, we pick the cˆv[i] that maximizes s(cˆv[i], c_j^w). However, this could result in a many-to-one or inconsistent mapping — multiple color texts might select the same visual appearance, or the selected (c_v, c_w) pair may not have mutual maximality. The process may not yield a consistent set of coherent (visual, text) pairs. While the intent is to find cross-modally aligned color representations, the current description does not ensure coherence or one-to-one alignment. The authors refer to Section B for pseudocode, but as reviewers we must assess what's presented. This ambiguity leaves the actual implementation unclear and could affect reproducibility. The use of 'similarly' masks a non-trivial algorithmic design choice.

comments.title: Missing normalization in aggregated MLM score for inference
comments.explanation: The notation '[1] _l_  _i_ =1' appears to be an OCR artifact for the averaging operator: (1/l) ∑_{i=1}^l. Assuming this correction, the score s(r) is defined as the average log-probability of predicting each token in the relation. This makes sense as a way to aggregate token-level MLM outputs. However, in standard practice, such scores are often left as sums (not averages) to preserve additive structure in log-likelihoods. But more critically, longer relations (with more tokens) will naturally have lower average log-probabilities due to more negative terms, even if per-token confidence is high. Thus, comparing s(r) across relations of different lengths may be biased. The authors introduce different l-specific NA relations and templates per l, so scoring is within fixed-length groups. That helps, but the normalization by 1/l still introduces a length normalization that might not be necessary or optimal. More importantly, the formula is written as if s(r) is an average, yet later it's used as a ranking score without discussion of length effects. While not clearly wrong, the choice of averaging is not justified, and potential bias in cross-length comparison is not addressed. Given that relation length varies and templates are separated by l, this could impact ranking fairness. The issue is subtle but affects interpretability of the inference mechanism.

comments.title: Inconsistent subscript indexing in CPS selection
comments.explanation: The notation uses both cˆv[i] and cˆjw, mixing i and j indices inconsistently. The outer set-builder quantifies over cˆv[i] ∈ Cˆv with index i, while the argmax is over cˆjw ∈ Cˆw with index j. This is acceptable if i is fixed in the scope of the argmax, but the binding is ambiguous. Worse, the resulting set is defined as {cw | cw = ...}, but uses cˆjw in the expression — implying that the selected color text may vary per i, yet the notation cw (without index) suggests a flat set. The correct interpretation is likely: for each i, find j that maximizes s(cˆv[i], cˆw[j]), and assign that cˆw[j] as the paired text for cˆv[i]. Then Cw collects all such selected texts. But the notation elides the pairing structure — it should probably be written as cw[i] = argmax_j s(cˆv[i], cˆw[j]), then Cw = {cw[i] | i ∈ [N]}. As written, it reads as if Cw is a set of texts each defined by a maximization over j, but indexed only by i in the condition. The mix of bound variables and unindexed cw is confusing and formally imprecise. Given that this defines a core component of the CPS algorithm, clarity is important. OCR likely preserved structure, so this reflects author notation choice.

comments.title: Misleading claim about transparency parameter
comments.quote: To make the raw content of the colored image regions available to VL-PTMs, a transparency hyperparameter _α ∈_ (0 _,_ 1) is further applied to color visual appearances in practice.
comments.explanation: This sentence implies that transparency (α) is introduced so that the model can still 'see' the content under the color overlay, which makes intuitive sense. However, this motivation appears in the middle of a section describing the CPS algorithm, which is focused on selecting semantic color pairs (c^v, c^w) based on model sensitivity. There is no mention of α in the CPS algorithm — is α fixed during search? Is it optimized? Does it affect the decoding score s(cˆv, cˆw)? The text gives no guidance. Applying transparency changes the actual visual input, so the CPS search should arguably be performed with α applied to ensure consistency. Otherwise, the 'optimal' colors found on opaque blocks may not generalize to semi-transparent overlays. The paper presents α as a practical trick but does not integrate it into the CPS framework. This creates a disconnect: the prompt search is done under one visual regime (fully colored blocks), but used in another (semi-transparent). The claim that α 'makes the raw content available' is plausible, but its interaction with CPS is underspecified and potentially undermines the optimality of the selected colors.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of subscript indices in color set definitions
comments.quote: C_w = \{c_w \mid c_w = \arg\max_{\hat{c}_j^w \in \hat{C}_w} s(\hat{c}_i^v, \hat{c}_j^w), \hat{c}_i^v \in \hat{C}_v\}
comments.explanation: At first glance, this equation appears problematic because the subscript $j$ on $\hat{c}_j^w$ in the argmax is used while $i$ indexes $\hat{c}_i^v$, but $i$ does not appear on the left-hand side or within the condition except as a bound variable. This raises concern about whether the definition depends on $i$, making $C_w$ a set per $i$, or if the indexing is inconsistent. Contextually, $C_w$ is later used as a fixed set of selected color texts, not indexed by $i$, suggesting a possible notational confusion. Re-examining the full context, it appears the authors intend to select, for each $\hat{c}_i^v$, the best color text $\hat{c}_j^w$, and collect all such $c_w$. But the way it's written implies $C_w$ is the set of $c_w$ such that $c_w = \arg\max_j s(\hat{c}_i^v, \hat{c}_j^w)$ for *some* $i$, but the scope of the quantifier over $i$ is unclear. A more standard way would be to write $C_w = \left\{ \arg\max_{\hat{c}_j^w} s(\hat{c}_i^v, \hat{c}_j^w) \mid \hat{c}_i^v \in \hat{C}_v \right\}$ with duplicates possibly removed. As written, the use of $c_w$ (without subscript) ambiguously conflates a singleton result of argmax with a set. While context resolves the intent—this is a candidate selection based on max decoding scores—the notation misleads by mixing indexed and unindexed variables. This could mislead a careful reader into thinking the selection is independent of $i$.

comments.title: Ambiguous grouping in reported accuracy improvements
comments.quote: CPT achieves 17 _._ 3% absolute accuracy improvement, and 73 _._ 8% relative standard deviation reduction on average with one shot in RefCOCO evaluation
comments.explanation: The claim reports two statistics — accuracy improvement and standard deviation reduction — in a single sentence, both 'on average with one shot in RefCOCO evaluation'. The text implies these improvements are comparative (vs. fine-tuning), but the reference for 'relative standard deviation reduction' is ambiguous: is it relative to the standard deviation of fine-tuning, or to zero? Given that the paper reports standard deviations in Table 1, and the reduction is described as 'relative', the intended interpretation is likely (std_fine-tuning - std_CPT)/std_fine-tuning. However, it's unclear over which subset the 'average' is computed — across datasets, splits, or test splits (val/testA/testB). From Table 1, for one-shot RefCOCO, the mean std for fine-tuning is roughly (4.9 + 7.6 + 8.4)/3 ≈ 7.0, and for CPT-Blk it’s (0.9 + 4.0 + 2.9)/3 ≈ 2.6, giving a reduction of ~62.8%, close to 73.8% but not exact. The discrepancy suggests either a different averaging scheme or a miscalculation. The paper does not specify how 'average' is computed. While minor OCR-style spacing (e.g., "17 _._ 3%") is harmless, this aggregation ambiguity affects interpretability of the headline metric. A careful reviewer might question whether the reported 73.8% is an arithmetic mean or over different models. Given that such reductions are central to the stability claim (Gao et al., 2021), the lack of specification is a weakness.

comments.title: Missing definition of visual sub-prompt transformation Ψ(·)
comments.quote: resulting in a set of colored image proposals Ψ( R ; C ), where Ψ( · ) denotes visual sub-prompt.
comments.explanation: The symbol Ψ is introduced without defining how it operates. It is described only as marking region proposals with unique colors from C. However, no details are provided about additive blending (e.g., via α transparency), shape (blocks vs. masks), or how overlapping regions are handled. The parameter α ∈ (0,1) is mentioned later in the context of CPS, but not linked back to Ψ. Since Ψ is used as a mathematical object, its omission of key methodological details — such as how color is superimposed, whether it replaces or overlays pixel values, and how α is applied — makes reproduction impossible without assumptions. This is especially problematic given that CPT-Seg and CPT-Blk exhibit different performance, indicating that the form of Ψ matters. While the prose later mentions transparency, the lack of functional definition (e.g., Ψ(v_i; c_i) = α * image(v_i) + (1−α) * c_i^v) prevents clarity. The abstract and methodology imply that visual prompts are crucial, yet the implementation is underspecified.

comments.title: Inconsistency between textual sub-prompt description and predicate classification template
comments.quote: Tr( s, o ) = [CLS] The sw in c[i]w color is [MASK] the ow in cjw color [SEP]
comments.explanation: In the passage, this template is used for predicate classification, but the form as written is ambiguous: it contains only one [MASK], yet later text says 'l consecutive masked tokens' are used. The example relation 'walking on' (l=2) cannot be reconstructed with one mask. This contradicts the claim that the template T(·; l) has l masked tokens. Furthermore, the text uses 'c[i]w' and 'cjw' — different subscript notation — while earlier notation uses j for index in candidate sets and i for visual appearance. This could be an OCR error, but even assuming 'ci^w' and 'cj^w', the template as given only allows one relation token. The authors likely intend T(s,o;l) = [CLS] The sw in ci^w color is [MASK_1] ... [MASK_l] the ow in cj^w color [SEP], but this is not clearly stated. This underspecification could mislead a reader into thinking all relations are single-token. While the section on inference defines s(r) with sum over l tokens, the template formulation must match. The inconsistency between the written template and variable-l requirement is a real flaw.

comments.title: Unclear treatment of NA relation during training for partial l matches
comments.quote: For templates where l = |r|, models are asked to reconstruct the NA relation. For (s,o) that do not have any relation in the image, models are asked to reconstruct the NA relation for all T(s,o;l).
comments.explanation: The second sentence is clear: when no relation exists, reconstruct NA for every l. But the first sentence says: 'For templates where l = |r|, models are asked to reconstruct the NA relation' — this contradicts the earlier training objective, which should reconstruct r (not NA) when (s,r,o) is a ground-truth triplet. This appears to be a typo: the intended meaning is likely 'For templates where l ≠ |r|' or 'for negative samples where no relation holds, and l = |r|'. As written, it suggests that whenever the template length matches the relation length, NA should be predicted — which makes no sense. The context resolves the intent: NA is a negative class. But the sentence misstates the condition. This would severely mislead a reader about training dynamics. Probably, the authors meant to write: 'For negative samples (s,o) without any relation, under templates T(·; l), models are asked to predict the NA relation of length l.' As written, the condition l = |r| depends on r, which is undefined for negative samples.

comments.title: Misalignment between color selection and final C definition
comments.quote: C = {(cv, cw) | cv = arg max_{\hat{c}_i^v \in \hat{C}_v} s(\hat{c}_i^v, c_j^w), c_j^w \in C_w}
comments.explanation: The first step defines $C_w$ as the set of color texts that maximize $s(\hat{c}_i^v, \hat{c}_j^w)$ over $j$, for each $i$. Then $C_w$ is fixed. The second step constructs $C$ by selecting, for each $c_j^w \in C_w$, the $\hat{c}_i^v$ that maximizes $s(\hat{c}_i^v, c_j^w)$. But the original definition of $C_w$ already used $\arg\max_j s(\hat{c}_i^v, \hat{c}_j^w)$ — meaning the top text for a given visual appearance. Now, going in reverse, the best visual appearance for that text may not be the original one. This asymmetry is not discussed. Worse, the process could result in duplicate pairings or omit candidates. A mutual consistency check or joint optimization would be more principled, but the paper presents it as a greedy two-step. While the CPS method is clearly intended to find high-scoring pairs, the bidirectional selection is not guaranteed to yield the best joint pairs. This could be functional, but the method lacks justification — why not perform a joint search? The lack of explanation for this two-stage asymmetric selection is a mild methodological weakness.

comments.title: Ambiguous reference to 'frequency-based baseline' without prior definition
comments.quote: Specifically, we compare colors obtained from the frequency-based baseline ( **Freq** ) (See Section 3.4)
comments.explanation: At first, I was confused because 'frequency-based baseline' wasn’t explicitly defined earlier in the paper. I checked Section 3.4, which mentions that a naive approach uses the most frequent color text in pre-training text as the color label, with its standard RGB. This matches the intuition behind 'Freq'. However, the term 'frequency-based baseline' appears only here and is not introduced earlier, despite being a key comparator. The reference to Section 3.4 is helpful but occurs after the label is introduced in parentheses, which reverses the typical order (define first, then cite). While the meaning can be reconstructed from context, a careful reader might momentarily think 'Freq' refers to something else—like frequency of region proposals—before resolving it. Given that this is a core comparison in the ablation study, the term should have been clearly defined when the naive method was first described in Section 3.4.

comments.title: Inconsistent shot settings reported in text versus evaluation protocol definition
comments.quote: all the following experiments are conducted based on CPT-Blk on the validation set of RefCOCO in 0 _,_ 2 _,_ 8 shot settings.
comments.explanation: The paper previously states in Section 4.1 that few-shot experiments use K = 1, 2, 4, 8, 16 shots. The text here references 0, 2, and 8-shot settings. While 0 and 8 are part of the standard setup, 2-shot is consistent but raises a concern: why skip 1-shot, which is used in the main Table 1 and emphasized in the abstract (e.g., 17.3% improvement with one shot)? More importantly, Figure 3a uses labels '0-Shot', '2-Shot', '8-Shot' in its x-axis, but the data in Table 1 includes 1-shot and not 2-shot for RefCOCO test splits. This creates a discrepancy: the ablation in Section 4.3 uses 2-shot where main results emphasize 1-shot. I checked whether 2-shot results are reported elsewhere—yes, in Table 1 under 'Few-Shot'—but the emphasis differs. The concern is not correctness, but consistency in focus: if 1-shot is the primary few-shot regime, why analyze color impact in 2-shot? The choice may be due to stability (2-shot being less noisy), but this isn't justified. Without explanation, it risks misleading readers about which results are representative.

comments.title: Color set mismatch in zoom-in study implies unexplained filtering
comments.quote: we merge the top-6 colors from the baseline and CPS, and remove the colors that are not included in the models’ complete color sets (e.g., _black \in C/_ in CPS).
comments.explanation: Initially, I was concerned that 'black' is in Freq but not in CPS's final color set, suggesting CPS may exclude some common colors. The example states _black \in C/_ (i.e., not in C), but this contradicts intuition since black is semantically salient. I checked Table 2: Freq includes (0,0,0), black; CPS does not. This is intentional—CPS selects based on decoding correlation, not frequency. However, the paper doesn’t explain why black was filtered out. Was it because the decoding score was low? Was it due to poor visual-textual alignment in VinVL? This omission matters because black is a strong baseline color and its exclusion could bias the comparison. The method section says CPS retains colors with highest decoding scores, but gives no threshold or mechanism for final set size. If CPS ends up with fewer than 6 colors, the 'top-6' claim is misleading. The zoom-in study only includes colors present in both, so the comparison is valid, but the filtering process is underspecified—readers can’t reproduce how many colors were initially considered or why some were dropped.

comments.title: Underspecified cross-modal prompt search (CPS) algorithm
comments.quote: we retain the color texts that achieve the largest decoding scores for visual appearance candidates: _Cw_ = _{cw|cw_ = arg max _c_ ˆ _jw[\in][C]_[ˆ] _w[s]_[(ˆ] _[c] v[i][,]_[ ˆ] _[c][j] w_[)] _[,]_[ ˆ] _[c][i] v[\in][C]_[ˆ] _[v][}]_[.][Similarly,][we][can] obtain the visual appearances according to the largest decoding score, resulting in the color set: _C_ = _{_ ( _cv, cw_ ) _|cv_ = arg max _c_ ˆ _iv\inC_ ˆ _v s_ (ˆ _c[i] v[, c][j] w_[)] _[, c][j] w[\inC][w][}]_
comments.explanation: At first glance, the two-step process seems clear: pick best text for each visual candidate, then best visual for each text. But deeper inspection reveals ambiguity. Is Cw fixed before selecting cv? If so, then the second step may pick a cv not in Ĉv? Or is it iterative? The pseudocode is referred to Section B (not in context), which raises red flags. Moreover, no mention is made of how many colors are selected: is |C| fixed to 6? 10? The algorithm must resolve ties and ensure one-to-one mapping. Also, if s(ĉvi, ĉjw) is asymmetric, the mutual best match isn't guaranteed. This affects reproducibility. For instance, if multiple visual candidates map to 'red', how is one chosen? The transparency hyperparameter α is mentioned but not its value or tuning method. These are critical implementation details. Given that CPS is one of the key contributions, this level of underspecification hinders reproducibility, especially for an algorithm claimed to be 'principled'.

comments.title: Inconsistency between Table 1 and text claim on CPT-Blk vs CPT-Seg in low-shot regimes
comments.quote: CPT-Seg (segmentation masks) yields better performance than CPT-Blk (colored blocks) in low-shot regimes due to more natural visual sub-prompts
comments.explanation: This is stated in the summary, but I wanted to verify against Table 1. In the 1-shot setting on RefCOCO val: CPT-Blk → 37.2±0.9, CPT-Seg → 41.5±1.5. Yes, CPT-Seg is clearly better. But on RefCOCO+ val: CPT-Blk → 37.9±4.0, CPT-Seg → 42.3±5.9 — still better. However, in 0-shot, RefCOCO: CPT-Blk 19.4±0.6, CPT-Seg 13.4±0.4 — here CPT-Blk is substantially better. The abstract and introduction emphasize performance gains in few- and zero-shot, but the claim 'CPT-Seg > CPT-Blk in low-shot' fails in zero-shot. So what counts as 'low-shot'? The paper doesn’t define it. If low-shot includes zero-shot, the claim is questionable. Perhaps the authors mean only few-shot (K≥1). But zero-shot is a key regime. The text says 'in low-shot regimes' generally, which readers will interpret as including zero-shot. Moreover, the reasoning ('more natural') doesn’t explain why Seg underperforms in zero-shot. This suggests the advantage of Seg may be data-dependent, not universal. The claim thus overgeneralizes. A more accurate claim would be 'CPT-Seg outperforms CPT-Blk in few-shot (≥1-shot) but not necessarily in zero-shot'.

comments.title: Figure 3b axis label ambiguity in zoom-in study
comments.quote: red blue green yellow pink purple red blue green yellow pink purple red blue green yellow pink purple
comments.explanation: Looking at Figure 3b, the x-axis lists colors three times (once per shot level), but the text says the figure is a 'zoom-in study of aligned individual colors'. I initially thought each group corresponds to a shot setting. But the colors are interleaved: for example, 'red' appears at positions where in Freq it might be present but not in CPS. Then I realized it's showing the full union of colors used in both models, with values only where the color exists. But the repetition of the same color labels across shot groups is misleading—it looks like three separate bars per color per shot, but it's actually one bar per (color, shot) if present. The figure layout suggests a 3×N grid, but it’s really a faceted bar plot with shared x-axis labels. A reader might think all colors are evaluated in all settings, but in fact some are missing (like black in CPS). This could lead to misinterpretation of comparative performance. The caption should clarify the sparsity and alignment logic. While not a critical error, it reduces clarity in a key analysis figure.

comments.title: Missing clarification on why 'none' token is needed only for batches
comments.quote: we further introduce a new candidate text _none_ in the decoding vocabulary, to indicate that there is no target region in the batch.
comments.explanation: This is mentioned in Section 3.4 under image region batching. My initial thought: why is 'none' only needed for batches? Couldn't a single full-image prompt work? But the context explains that regions are batched due to limited color set size and overlap issues. So each batch is a local prompt. If the target is not in the batch, the model must say 'none'. This makes sense. But the text doesn’t clarify whether 'none' is also used during full supervision or only in few/zero-shot. Also, is 'none' included in the color set C? No—it’s a special token. But its embedding must be part of the MLM vocabulary. The paper doesn’t say whether 'none' is trainable or fixed. Given that CPT reuses the MLM head without new parameters, introducing a new token seems to violate that principle—unless 'none' is already in the pre-trained vocabulary. It likely is, but the concern is whether it’s ever used during pre-training in a similar context. If not, the model may not handle it well. The method doesn’t explain how 'none' is initialized or whether it’s fine-tuned. This is a small but potentially impactful design choice that’s left underspecified.

comments.title: Inconsistent Evaluation Protocols for Predicate Classification
comments.quote: Following previous works (Xu et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2019), we use recall@N (R@N) and mean recall@N (mR@N) as the evaluation metrics. During training, K labeled instances are provided for each relation.
comments.explanation: At first glance, this appears consistent with standard practice in predicate classification, where R@N and mR@N are indeed common. The paper's summary confirms these metrics (R@50, R@100, mR@50, mR@100) are used for Visual Genome. However, the sentence 'During training, K labeled instances are provided for each relation' raises concern: it suggests a balanced few-shot setting per relation, which contradicts the later claim about a distribution gap between balanced training and long-tailed test set. While this is explicitly acknowledged in the 'Results' section as an explanation for micro/macro performance discrepancy, the issue lies in how it's presented. The phrasing implies a standard few-shot setup, but in fact, it's a per-relation K-shot setup, which is more stringent and less commonly reported. The problem is not the design, but the underspecification: it doesn't clarify that 'K shots' means exactly K labeled triplets per relation, which is crucial for reproducibility. This risks misleading readers into thinking it's a total K-shot setting. Although context later explains the long-tail issue, the method description fails to define this key experimental choice.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Baseline Description for Predicate Classification
comments.quote: We adopt fine-tuning of VinVL as our most direct baseline model. Specifically, we feed the image regions and their categories into the model, and concatenate the visual hidden representations of the subject and object. Then the object pair representation is fed into a softmax classifier.
comments.explanation: The description of the fine-tuning baseline seems straightforward, but upon closer inspection, it lacks critical details. The authors correctly align the baseline with their method by using object categories and region features, which is appropriate. However, the phrase 'feed the image regions and their categories' is ambiguous: does this mean only region features and category names are used, or is the full image processed with region proposals? More importantly, in standard Visual Genome setups, bounding box coordinates or spatial features are often included. The omission of whether spatial coordinates or relative geometry are used is significant — these are known to strongly impact performance in relation detection. While the authors state 'all VL-PTMs are in base size,' which matches common practice, the baseline architecture lacks specification of inputs and fusion method beyond concatenation. Given that CPT's advantage partly stems from data efficiency, an underspecified baseline risks unfair comparison. The context does not clarify this in other sections, making reproduction difficult.

comments.title: Discrepancy Between Claimed and Stated Fully Supervised Baseline Data Size
comments.quote: We also report the results of strong baselines that are tailored for the task, and are fully supervised with 315 _,_ 642 labeled triplets, including Neural Motif (Zellers et al., 2018), BGNN (Li et al., 2021a), PCPL (Yan et al., 2020) and DT2-ACBS (Desai et al., 2021).
comments.explanation: The number '315,642' labeled triplets is stated precisely, which initially suggests strong empirical grounding. However, the paper's summary mentions that CPT-Blk is competitive in the fully supervised setting, and the claim is that CPT works well even there. The issue arises when considering typical Visual Genome statistics: the number of annotated relationships varies widely depending on preprocessing (e.g., filtering rare relations), but 315k is plausible for the full dataset. The problem is not the number itself, but the lack of clarification whether this number refers to the same processed dataset used in the CPT experiments. If the baselines were trained on the full Visual Genome while CPT used a filtered or subset version (e.g., top 50 relations), the comparison would be invalid. The section says 'we adopt the popular Visual Genome dataset... which contains 50 visual relations,' indicating a 50-relation subset. Thus, the baselines' training on 315k triplets must also be on the same filtered set. However, it's questionable whether models like Neural Motif or DT2-ACBS were reported in their original papers on this specific 50-relation subset. Without confirming that the baseline results are re-implemented or re-reported under the same setting, this risks comparing against out-of-setting numbers, undermining the claim of competitiveness. This could be a minor framing issue if all baselines were fairly re-evaluated, but as written, it appears potentially misleading.

comments.title: Unexplained Performance Drop in Micro Metrics for Few-Shot Settings
comments.quote: We note that while the macro performance of CPT monotonically increases as the shot number grows, the micro performance drops first in 1- and 4-shot settings. This is due to the distribution gap between the balanced training set (i.e., K shot for each relation) and the long-tail test set.
comments.explanation: The explanation given—distribution gap between balanced training and long-tailed test—is reasonable and well-known in few-shot learning. However, the claim that micro performance 'drops first' is puzzling. Micro metrics typically aggregate globally (total true positives over total positives), so if performance increases with more data, micro should generally improve. A drop in low-shot micro performance could occur if rare classes dominate the test set and the model initially performs well on them due to pre-training, but early fine-tuning harms generalization. While the authors mention that pre-training follows a long-tail distribution, helping zero-shot performance on frequent relations, the explanation for why micro recall *drops* in 1- and 4-shot needs more nuance. Is micro calculated per-relation then averaged? The summary defines mR@50/100 as mean recall, which is macro-averaged. This raises a red flag: if 'micro performance' refers to overall recall (micro-averaged), a drop with more data contradicts typical behavior unless there's negative transfer. But if 'micro' here is a misnomer and they mean performance on rare classes, the terminology is incorrect. Given that mR@N is defined as *mean* recall, the use of 'micro' is likely a mistake. This is a real ambiguity: the authors may mean 'performance on infrequent relations' but used 'micro' incorrectly, which could mislead careful readers about evaluation practices.

comments.title: Missing Justification for NA Relation Tokenization
comments.quote: In our experiments, the NA relation is _irrelevant_ , _no relation_ , _no relation with_ for _l_ = 1 _,_ 2 _,_ 3 respectively.
comments.explanation: This design choice is practical and makes sense: using multi-token fillers for the NA class that match the relation length ensures fair scoring. The authors correctly align NA with l tokens. However, no justification is given for the specific choice of phrases. Why 'no relation with' and not 'has no relation with' or similar? More importantly, these phrases may not be semantically equivalent or equally likely under the MLM head. For example, 'irrelevant' is an adjective and may not fit the context 'The X in red color is [MASK] the Y in blue color' as naturally as 'not holding'. This could introduce bias in scoring, where NA is either too easy or too hard to predict. While the approach is sound in principle, the lack of ablation or discussion on NA phrasing risks hiding a confounding factor in performance. The context doesn't address this in later sections, so it remains an underspecified methodological choice that could affect fairness of comparison, especially in low-data regimes.

progressive__glm-4.6.label: Progressive (glm-4.6)
progressive__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: This paper is well-written and presents a timely, intuitive, and potentially impactful idea. The clarity is excellent; the authors effectively articulate the critical "objective gap" between pre-training and fine-tuning in vision-language models and propose a clever solution in Cross-modal Prompt Tuning (CPT). The core concept of using color-based co-referential markers to reformulate visual grounding as a fill-in-the-blank problem is both novel and well-motivated. However, the most significant issue is the extraordinary magnitude of the empirical claims. The reported improvements (e.g., over 17% absolute accuracy gain with only one shot) are so substantial that they immediately raise questions about the experimental setup, the strength of the baselines, and the reproducibility of the results. To be convincing, the paper must provide exceptionally strong evidence, including meticulous ablation studies to disentangle the contribution of the prompt design from other factors, a clear definition of what constitutes the "one-shot" and "zero-shot" settings, and a rigorous comparison against state-of-the-art few-shot methods. While the conceptual contribution is strong, the paper's ultimate credibility will depend entirely on the robustness and validity of these surprisingly high-performance figures.

comments.title: Underspecified image region batching strategy
comments.quote: Therefore, we divide the image regions into batches, where each batch contains a handful of moderately overlapping image regions, and mark each batch with a visual sub-prompt respectively.
comments.explanation: The paper introduces image region batching as a solution to handle more regions than available colors, but provides insufficient details for reproducibility. Key aspects remain underspecified: (1) What constitutes 'a handful' - is this 3, 5, or another specific number? (2) How is 'moderately overlapping' quantified or measured? (3) What is the algorithm for assigning regions to batches? (4) How are batches processed sequentially or in parallel? These details are crucial for understanding the method's behavior and implementing it correctly. The vague description makes it difficult to assess the impact of batching on performance or to reproduce the results.

comments.title: Missing transparency parameter α specification
comments.quote: To make the raw content of the colored image regions available to VL-PTMs, a transparency hyperparameter α ∈ (0, 1) is further applied to color visual appearances in practice.
comments.explanation: The transparency parameter α is introduced as important for making raw image content available, but no specific value or range is provided. This is a critical hyperparameter that balances between the color marking and preserving original image features. Without specifying: (1) What value of α was used in experiments, (2) How α is applied (e.g., alpha blending, weighted averaging), (3) How α was selected or tuned, (4) The sensitivity of results to different α values, readers cannot properly interpret or reproduce the results. This omission is particularly problematic given that α directly affects how much of the original image content is visible through the color overlay.

comments.title: Undefined template notation and incomplete definition
comments.quote: Specifically, the query text q (e.g., "the horse watched by the woman") is transformed into a fill-in-the-blank query using a template Tg (·) as:
comments.explanation: The template function T_g(·) is used to transform query text but is not properly defined before use. While the general concept of templates is understandable, the specific notation T_g(·) appears without explanation of: (1) What the subscript g represents, (2) Whether T_g is a fixed template or a learnable function, (3) The exact form of the template being used. Additionally, the text ends with 'as:' but no template follows, likely due to an OCR extraction issue. This creates ambiguity about the implementation details of the textual sub-prompt component, making it difficult to reproduce the exact prompt format used.

comments.title: Insufficient justification for cross-modal prompt search method
comments.quote: To address the challenge, we present a principled cross-modal prompt search (CPS) algorithm for CPT, which jointly considers visual and textual semantics in real-world cross-modal data.
comments.explanation: The paper presents a cross-modal prompt search method but lacks rigorous justification for why this specific approach is optimal. The method selects colors based on decoding scores from pure colored blocks, but the paper doesn't: (1) Provide theoretical justification for why this approach works better than simpler alternatives, (2) Analyze the computational complexity or scalability of the search method, (3) Explain why pseudo-data instances with pure colored blocks are representative of real-world scenarios. The claim that this is 'principled' needs more substantiation.

comments.title: Ambiguous implementation of special tokens ('none' and NA relation)
comments.quote: To handle the batches that do not contain the target region, we further introduce a new candidate text none in the decoding vocabulary, to indicate that there is no target region in the batch. For each template T(·; l), we introduce a special NA relation consisting of l tokens, which indicates that there is no relation between the entity pair under T(·; l).
comments.explanation: The introduction of special tokens for negative cases lacks sufficient implementation details. For 'none': (1) How is it integrated into the vocabulary - during training or inference only? (2) Is the model specifically trained to recognize 'none'? (3) What happens when 'none' is predicted? For NA relation: (1) Why were specific phrases ('irrelevant', 'no relation', 'no relation with') chosen for different l values? (2) Are these fixed or learnable? (3) How does the model learn to output NA for non-existent relations? This ambiguity makes it difficult to understand the complete inference and training pipelines.

comments.title: Duplicate condition in predicate classification training
comments.quote: (1) For templates where l = |r| , models are asked to reconstruct the ith masked token in T(s, o; l) with the ith relational token ri using the MLM head. (2) For templates where l = |r| , since there is no relation between (s, o) under T(s, o; l), models are asked to reconstruct the NA relation.
comments.explanation: The training procedure for predicate classification contains a clear error where both conditions (1) and (2) specify 'l = |r|' but describe contradictory actions. In case (1), the model reconstructs the relational tokens, while in case (2), it reconstructs the NA relation. Based on the context, case (2) should likely specify 'l ≠ |r|' to handle templates with a different number of masked tokens than the actual relation. This error undermines the clarity of the training methodology for predicate classification.

comments.title: Contradiction between text and table for 16-shot RefCOCO+ results
comments.quote: We note that CPT-Blk slightly underperforms fine-tuning with 16 shots in RefCOCO+ evaluation.
comments.explanation: In Section 4.2, point (4), the authors state that CPT-Blk slightly underperforms fine-tuning with 16 shots in RefCOCO+. However, looking at Table 1, for RefCOCO+ with 16 shots, CPT-Blk achieves 64.4% while fine-tuning achieves 64.1%, meaning CPT-Blk actually outperforms fine-tuning. This is a direct contradiction between the textual claim and the reported results.

comments.title: Inconsistent and incomplete performance claims about CPT variants
comments.quote: We note that CPT-Blk slightly outperforms CPT-Seg in the fully supervised setting, and we refer readers to Section A.3 for a detailed analysis. Coloring objects with segmentation masks in visual sub-prompts (CPT-Seg) achieves even better results than blocks (CPT-Blk).
comments.explanation: The authors make multiple imprecise or contradictory claims about CPT-Blk vs CPT-Seg performance: (1) The claim that CPT-Blk slightly outperforms CPT-Seg in fully supervised setting is too general - this is only true for RefCOCO+ (74.4% vs 73.9%), while CPT-Seg significantly outperforms CPT-Blk in RefCOCO (87.2% vs 81.9%). (2) The claim that CPT-Seg achieves better results than CPT-Blk ignores the zero-shot setting where CPT-Seg underperforms in RefCOCO (30.3% vs 32.2%). These inconsistencies should be corrected to provide accurate reporting.

comments.title: Undefined performance metrics
comments.quote: while the macro performance of CPT monotonically increases as the shot number grows, the micro performance drops first in 1- and 4-shot settings.
comments.explanation: The authors introduce 'macro performance' and 'micro performance' without defining these terms in the context of predicate classification. While these terms might be standard in relation classification (typically referring to performance across all relations vs. per-relation averages), this ambiguity could mislead readers unfamiliar with this specific terminology. Clear definitions are needed for proper interpretation of the results.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about pre-training distribution
comments.quote: Since the relations in the pre-training corpora also follow a long-tail distribution, CPT can achieve a high starting point for micro performance.
comments.explanation: The authors state that relations in pre-training corpora follow a long-tail distribution without providing evidence or citation. This claim is used to explain CPT's high starting point for micro performance, but without supporting evidence, this explanation remains speculative. Either empirical evidence or citations should be provided to substantiate this claim.

comments.title: Incomplete baseline comparison in results
comments.quote: CPT significantly outperforms the random baseline and the strong fine-tuning baseline in zero-shot and few-shot settings.
comments.explanation: The authors claim CPT outperforms baselines but only provide specific numbers for the fine-tuning baseline comparison ('outperforming fine-tuning by 13.6% absolute points'). No numbers are given for the random baseline comparison, making it impossible to verify the full claim. Additionally, it's unclear whether 'strong fine-tuning baseline' refers to the VinVL fine-tuning mentioned earlier or a different method. Complete baseline comparisons with specific numbers are needed.

comments.title: Unclear experimental setup parameters
comments.quote: During training, K labeled instances are provided for each relation... using 32 shots, CPT achieves a strong mR@100 of 37.7%
comments.explanation: The experimental setup mentions 'K labeled instances are provided for each relation' but the results refer to specific shot numbers (e.g., '32 shots'). The relationship between K and these shot numbers is unclear. Is K=32 in the 32-shot setting? This ambiguity affects the reproducibility of the experiments. The paper should clarify how K relates to the shot numbers reported in the results.

Based on the context, this should be ci = (c[i]v, c[i]w) where c[i]v is visual appearance and c[i]w is color text. This appears to be an OCR error where the 'w' subscript was misread as 'v' in the tuple definition. While the intent is clear from context, the notation as written is technically incorrect.

For the 1-shot few-shot setting, the first entry under Shot is '1', but for other shot counts (2, 4, 8, 16), the first entry is 'Fine-tuning'. This makes the table harder to read and understand. While the bars appear to be grouped in sets of six (matching the six colors mentioned in the text), the labeling makes it difficult to interpret the specific performance of each color.

Given that this appears to be a fundamental limitation of the color-based approach (especially when the query text itself contains color terms), it would benefit from more thorough discussion of its implications and frequency. progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The proposed paper is well-motivated, addressing a critical bottleneck in the utility of Vision-Language Pre-Trained Models (VL-PTMs): the discrepancy between pre-training objectives and downstream fine-tuning tasks. The core insight—reformulating visual grounding as a "fill-in-the-blank" masked language modeling problem via coordinate-based color markers—is both creative and elegant. The writing is clear, the contribution is well-defined, and the reported few-shot performance gains are compelling. However, a significant potential issue lies in the methodology's reliance on "color" as a semantic bridge. While the authors present this as a solution to the objective gap, the paper needs to rigorously justify whether the model’s performance is truly derived from generalized cross-modal grounding or potentially from the model's existing biases toward color-word associations in its pre-training corpus. Furthermore, while the technical approach is sound, the leap from a conceptual "colorful" prompt to a robust, scalable system for diverse visual scenes requires detailed discussion regarding how the "color-based query template" handles scene complexity (e.g., overlapping objects or occlusions) to ensure the method is not just a clever trick for simple datasets, but a generalizable mechanism for visual grounding.

comments.title: Unclear Definition of Relative Standard Deviation Reduction
comments.quote: 73 _._ 8% relative standard deviation reduction
comments.explanation: The authors report a '73.8% relative standard deviation reduction' but fail to define what is being measured (accuracy, IoU, or model variance across runs). As accuracy is the primary metric, the inclusion of an unexplained standard deviation metric is confusing for the reader.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'Zero-shot' Definition for Supervised Tasks
comments.quote: zero-shot visual grounding independent of object types
comments.explanation: The term 'zero-shot' is used to describe a method that relies on test-time 'color-based co-referential marker' prompt engineering. The authors should clarify if this implies zero exposure to the dataset labels or if it acknowledges reliance on the specific color-masking paradigm as a form of supervision.

comments.title: Missing Equations and Opaque Definition of Visual/Textual Sub-prompts
comments.quote: Specifically, the query text $q$ ... is transformed into a fill-in-the-blank query using a template $T_g(\cdot)$ ... the notation $\Psi( R; C )$ is introduced to denote the 'visual sub-prompt'
comments.explanation: The text omits critical equations for $T_g(\cdot)$ and the probability calculation, making the core methodology non-reproducible. Simultaneously, $\Psi( R; C )$ is used without defining how visual markers are applied (e.g., overlay vs. attention modulation), hindering technical assessment.

comments.title: Ambiguous/Inconsistent Logic in Predicate Classification Training
comments.quote: (2) For templates where l = |r| , since there is no relation between ( s, o ) under T ( s, o ; l ), models are asked to reconstruct the NA relation.
comments.explanation: The training objective contains a logical contradiction where the condition for the 'NA' relation is listed as $l = |r|$, which is the identical condition used for positive relation reconstruction in point (1). Additionally, the definition of $l$-length masked tokens and the implementation of 'NA' relations (e.g., tokenizing 'no relation') are insufficiently specified.

comments.title: Formal Notation Errors in Cross-modal Prompt Search
comments.quote: Cw = {cw|cw = arg max c^ jw∈C^ w s(c iv, c jw)}
comments.explanation: The set builder notation is formalistically incorrect, as 'arg max' returns a single element (or ties), not a set. Furthermore, index binding for $i$ and $j$ in the selection process is ambiguous within the provided definition, making the color selection criteria for predicate classification difficult to interpret.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Table 1 Data
comments.quote: 1 Fine-tuning CPT-Blk CPT-Seg ... (missing values for RefCOCOg)
comments.explanation: Table 1 omits values for the RefCOCOg dataset across all shot categories. This contradicts the claims in Section 4.2 that CPT was evaluated on this dataset, rendering the performance claim unverified.

comments.title: Potential Overstatement of Zero-Shot Capabilities
comments.quote: CPT enables strong few-shot and even zero-shot visual grounding capabilities...
comments.explanation: The abstract claims 'strong' zero-shot performance, but empirical results for predicate classification show performance (1.8% R@50) closer to random chance. This conflates success in simple Referring Expression Comprehension with the much more complex task of predicate classification.

comments.title: Inconsistency in RefCOCO+ Evaluation Claim
comments.quote: However, this problem can be alleviated with more tuning instances in the fully supervised scenario
comments.explanation: The authors claim that full supervision alleviates CPT underperformance on RefCOCO+, but Table 1 shows that even in the fully supervised scenario, CPT still performs worse than the fine-tuning baseline, contradicting the author's stated conclusion.

comments.title: Ambiguous Normalization in Relation Scoring
comments.quote: s(r) = \frac{1}{l} \sum_{i=1}^{l} \log P([MASK]_i = r_i | T(s, o; l))
comments.explanation: Normalizing the log-likelihood by the relation length $l$ introduces a potential bias toward shorter relations that is not theoretically justified or explained in the text. This scoring method requires clarification to confirm if it inadvertently weights predicted relations based on length.

progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.label: Progressive Original (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.overall_feedback: The proposed paper is well-motivated, addressing a critical bottleneck in the utility of Vision-Language Pre-Trained Models (VL-PTMs): the discrepancy between pre-training objectives and downstream fine-tuning tasks. The core insight—reformulating visual grounding as a "fill-in-the-blank" masked language modeling problem via coordinate-based color markers—is both creative and elegant. The writing is clear, the contribution is well-defined, and the reported few-shot performance gains are compelling. However, a significant potential issue lies in the methodology's reliance on "color" as a semantic bridge. While the authors present this as a solution to the objective gap, the paper needs to rigorously justify whether the model’s performance is truly derived from generalized cross-modal grounding or potentially from the model's existing biases toward color-word associations in its pre-training corpus. Furthermore, while the technical approach is sound, the leap from a conceptual "colorful" prompt to a robust, scalable system for diverse visual scenes requires detailed discussion regarding how the "color-based query template" handles scene complexity (e.g., overlapping objects or occlusions) to ensure the method is not just a clever trick for simple datasets, but a generalizable mechanism for visual grounding.

comments.title: Unclear Definition of Relative Standard Deviation Reduction
comments.quote: 73 _._ 8% relative standard deviation reduction
comments.explanation: The authors report a '73.8% relative standard deviation reduction'. It is unclear whether this refers to the reduction in the standard deviation of accuracy across different samples/trials, or something else. Given that the primary metric in RefCOCO evaluation is usually 'accuracy@1' or 'IoU', 'Standard Deviation' is an unusual metric to highlight in an abstract. Without a clear definition of what is being measured (accuracy, IoU, or model variance across runs), this metric is difficult to interpret. While the authors define the accuracy improvement clearly, this secondary metric lacks context.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'Zero-shot' Definition for Supervised Tasks
comments.quote: zero-shot visual grounding independent of object types
comments.explanation: The authors claim CPT enables 'zero-shot visual grounding'. In the context of the paper, the method uses a 'color-based co-referential marker' paradigm which requires an image and a textual query. If no labeled pairs are provided during tuning, the VL-PTM relies on its pre-trained MLM head to bridge the color-marked image and the text. This is a clever use of the pre-trained weights, but calling it 'zero-shot' can be ambiguous. It effectively performs the task without task-specific training, but it relies on external prompt engineering (the colored masks). The authors should eventually clarify if 'zero-shot' implies no exposure to the RefCOCO dataset validation/test sets or just no supervised training on triplets.

comments.title: Under-specified Formatting of Numbers
comments.quote: 17 _._ 3% absolute accuracy improvement, and 73 _._ 8% relative standard deviation reduction
comments.explanation: The OCR has introduced spaces around the decimal points (e.g., '17 _._ 3%'). While the prompt instructions suggest being lenient with OCR-related issues, the academic rigor of the paper's claims relies on these specific numbers. A careful reader might be confused as to whether these are specific values or if the text was meant to be '17.3%'. I am flagging this as a minor concern—not because of the OCR output itself, but because the authors should verify these precision values in the final document to ensure they are interpreted correctly.

comments.title: Missing Equations in Textual Sub-prompt Section
comments.quote:
Specifically, the query text $q$ (e.g., “ the horse watched by the woman ”) is transformed into a fill-in-the-blank query using a template $T_g$ ( $·$ ) as: 

In this way, VL-PTMs are prompted to decide the color of which region is more appropriate to fill in the mask (e.g., red or blue ) as follows: 

where $v$ is the target region, $\mathbf{c} _{[i] w}$is the embedding of $c_{[i] w}$ in the pre-trained MLM head.
comments.explanation: The text abruptly breaks off after the colons where one would expect the definition of the template $T_g(\cdot)$ and the probability equation. While the context provides enough to understand the intent, the missing equations are a significant oversight in a technical paper, as the exact formulation of the template and the probability calculation are the core of the CPT methodology. This leaves the reader unable to reproduce the specific prompt formatting.

comments.title: Unexplained variables in predicate classification
comments.quote: The template $T_r ( s, o ) = [CLS] The $s_w$ in $c_{[i] w}$ color is [MASK] the $o_w$ in $c_{[j] w}$ color [SEP] where $s_w$ is the subject text, $o_w$ is the object text, and $c_{[i] w}$ and $c_{[j] w}$ are the corresponding color texts.
comments.explanation: In Section 3.5, the authors define $T_r(s, o)$, but the formula implies the masked token is singular ([MASK]). However, the subsequent sentence mentions 'accommodate the varied number of tokens' and 'template $T( \cdot ; l )$ will have $l$ consecutive masked tokens'. The gap between the singular [MASK] shown in the template definition and the later $l$-length requirement creates ambiguity about how the template is dynamically constructed, especially regarding how the model handles varying $l$ lengths within the same framework without changing the prompt structure.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of visual sub-prompt operation
comments.quote: Then we mark each region proposal $v_i$ in the image with a unique color $c[i] v$ for grounding, resulting in a set of colored image proposals $\Psi( R; C )$
comments.explanation: The notation $\Psi( R; C )$ is introduced to denote the 'visual sub-prompt', but it is never defined how the transformation $v_i \to c_{[i]v}$ is applied to the image pixels or feature map. Since this is the fundamental method of the paper, the lack of a formal definition regarding whether this is an overlay, a replacement, or a masked attention modulation makes it difficult to assess how this avoids interfering with the visual encoder's ability to 'see' the object content.

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in predicate classification training objective
comments.quote: (2) For templates where l = |r| , since there is no relation between ( s, o ) under T ( s, o ; l ), models are asked to reconstruct the NA relation.
comments.explanation: In point (2) of the training logic, the text states 'For templates where l = |r|', which appears to be a typo for 'l ≠ |r|'. The context clearly defines the scenario where the template length does not match the actual relation length, and thus the model should be penalized/trained to predict a 'NA' relation. As written, it implies the condition where the template length matches the relation length also results in predicting 'NA', which contradicts the preceding point (1) where the model reconstructs the actual relationtokens.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of the cross-modal prompt search selection criteria
comments.quote: retain the color texts that achieve the largest decoding scores for visual appearance candidates: Cw = {cw|cw = arg max cˆ jw∈Cˆ w s(ˆ c iv, ˆ c jw), ˆ c iv∈Cˆ v}
comments.explanation: The notation for the set Cw is formally problematic. The term 'arg max' over cˆ jw∈Cˆ w yields a single color text for a given cˆ iv, but the set builder notation implies a set construction dependent on ˆ c iv ∈ Cˆ v. As written, the set Cw would only contain a single best color text (or possibly a few if there are ties), rather than a set of optimal candidates. It is likely the authors intended to collect the set of top-k colors or simply meant to define Cw as the union of these argmax results, but the current expression is not a well-defined set builder notation.

comments.title: Inconsistency between text and formal notation for relation scoring
comments.quote: s ( r ) =[1] l  i =1 log [ P] [( [ [MASK] ] i = r i | T ( s, o ; l ))]
comments.explanation: The score is defined as an average (indicated by the 1/l factor outside the sum), but the text and notation could be interpreted differently depending on whether the bracket [1] is an artifact of OCR or a intended scalar. Assuming it corresponds to 1/l, it is standard, but the placement of symbols like [ P] and brackets makes it difficult to distinguish if it is a formal average of log-probabilities or a sum. Given the context of token-wise reconstruction, an average is correct, but the notation is visually broken by inclusions of fragments like '=[1]'.

comments.quote:
